data.title: UTF Rights Offering Strategy
data.description:
Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund's (NYSE:UTF) rights offering is underway. From the press release:

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF) (the "Fund") announced today that its Board of Directors has approved the terms of the issuance of transferable rights ("Rights") to the holders of the Fund's common stock (par value $0.001 per share), as of the record date, September 22, 2025 (the "Record Date"). Common stockholders on the Record Date ("Record Date Stockholders") will receive one transferable right for each share of common stock owned. Five Rights are required to purchase one newly issued share of common stock at the Subscription Price (defined below). The Subscription Price will be determined by the Fund upon the expiration of the Offer, which is currently expected to be October 16, 2025, unless the Fund extends the Offer (the "Expiration Date"). The Subscription Price will be based upon a formula equal to 95% of the average of the last reported sales price of a share of common stock on the NYSE on the Expiration Date, and each of the four preceding trading days (the "Formula Price"). If, however, the Formula Price is less than 90% of the net asset value at the close of trading on the Expiration Date, then the Subscription Price will be 90% of the Fund's net asset value on the Expiration Date. The Rights are expected to trade "when issued" on the NYSE beginning on September 19, 2025, and the Fund's shares of common stock are expected to trade "ex-Rights" on the NYSE beginning on September 22, 2025. The Rights are expected to begin trading for normal settlement on the NYSE (NYSE:UTF RT) on or about September 25, 2025.

Briefly, this is a transferable 1-for-5 rights offering with a subscription price set as the higher of 90% of NAV on the expiration date or 95% of the average closing price over the final five trading days. This "higher of" mechanism prevents a vicious cycle of widening discounts during the offering period, effectively creating a discount floor to limit NAV dilution. However, you should not subscribe if UTF's discount widens significantly (e.g., wider than -10%), as it would then be cheaper to buy shares directly on the open market. The ex-rights date was September 22, 2025, and the expiry date is October 16, 2025.

The stimulus for Cohen & Steers pulling the trigger was likely due to UTF's premium, which had been hovering in the mid-single digits for the last several months. Remember that issuing new shares at a premium is accretive, benefiting all shareholders, regardless of whether they subscribe or not.

Unfortunately, what usually happens is that investors, upon seeing the rights offering announcement, sell the fund, causing the price to drop and turning the premium into a discount. This is often driven by fears of dilution, therefore creating a "vicious cycle" where a wider discount will cause the offering to become more dilutive, thus putting further pressure on the fund and causing the discount to widen even more. However, as mentioned above, the -10% discount floor built into the rights offering helps limit maximum dilution and caps the NAV impact, even if the discount widens during the offering period.

Nevertheless, the fund is still in better shape than if it had announced the rights offering while trading at a discount, because it at least demonstrates some manager alignment with shareholder interests by timing the offering at a premium, which reduces potential dilution.

UTF sold off soon after the announcement of the rights offering, dropping from a small premium to its present -3.71% discount. This is why we often recommend a "sell and rebuy" approach for investors: selling the fund when the rights offering is first announced and then buying back towards the end of the rights offering. Often, the "free shares" gained through this rotation outweigh the potential benefits of subscribing for new shares at a discount, especially when factoring in the decline in value of existing shares as the discount widens (tax issues not considered). To reduce market risk, one could swap the funds into a similar infrastructure-focused CEF or ETF, such as the abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI) or the iShares Global Infrastructure ETF (IGF). This strategy helps maintain sector exposure while allowing investors to capitalize on the premium/discount dynamics of the fund that is undergoing the rights offering.

I expect the discount to widen a few more percentage points during the course of the offering, though likely not reaching the -10% discount floor, thanks to UTF's strong track record and solid management. On the NAV side, two factors may help limit dilution from UTF's rights offering. First, this is a 1-for-5 rights offering rather than the more common 1-for-3, meaning the fund aims to increase its share count (and AUM) by a maximum of 20%, rather than 33.3%. As a result, even if the offered shares reach the maximum discount of -10%, the NAV/share dilution would be limited to -1.7%, compared to -2.5% for a 1-for-3 offering. Second, all offering expenses, including sales commissions, will be fully covered by the advisor rather than the fund, making it different from other funds that often pass on the sales load and other offering costs to shareholders.

UTF is a well-established global infrastructure fund with a long track record of performance and was previously a holding in our Income Generator portfolio. If UTF's discount widens sufficiently, we might consider re-establishing a position in this excellent fund.

It is worth noting, however, that UTF has experienced extended periods of trading at a deep discount in the past (as wide as -15%). Investors should keep this in mind when evaluating potential entry points.

Action plan

In summary, here's a basic action plan for UTF investors:

  1. Subscribe if:

    • The discount remains narrower than -10% at expiry.
    • You want to increase your position and believe in UTF long-term.

  2. Do NOT subscribe if:

    • The discount widens beyond -10% at expiry.
    • It would be cheaper to buy shares directly on the open market instead.

  3. If you don't want to subscribe:

    • Sell your rights as soon as possible on the open market, as the value of the rights may potentially decline as the offering period progresses.

data.title: EssilorLuxottica: Neutral Stance Maintained Despite Encouraging Quarter
data.description:
We return to provide our views on EssilorLuxottica (OTCPK:ESLOF) (OTCPK:ESLOY), after having skipped our usual Q2 update, also in light of the recent announcements made together with Meta (META). For our new readers, the company is a fully integrated player in designing, manufacturing, and distributing sunglasses, lenses, and frames.

EssilorLuxottica was previously rated as a buy given its business evolution from a pure eyewear champion into a broader health-technology player. Management is channeling resources into three promising verticals (and three key sectors)—smart glasses, vision-enhancing hearing solutions, and myopia control (Tech, Pharma, and Fashion). Aside from the company's cash cow business, these initiatives addressed long-term structural themes, including digital connectivity and preventive eye care. With a strong brand, extensive retail network (GrandVision acquisition), and deep optical know-how, the company continues to have a competitive edge. That said, since April 2024, we have downgraded the company to neutral, mindful of risks such as intensifying competition and a pricey valuation.

In our last update, using a 28x P/E multiple, we derived a fair value of €267.4 per share. With the stock currently trading at around €280, the market has moved above our neutral valuation benchmark.

Q2 Review And Our Neutral View

As in previous quarters, we review the Q2 figures with our assessment, which continues to support a neutral stance on estimates.

Q2 company total top-line sales reached €7.17 million, with positive contributions in the Professional Solutions (up +3.9%) and Direct to Consumer (up +10.4%). By geographical region, all regions were positive, with support from EMEA up by 9.1%, LatAm by 8.2%, APAX by 7.8%, and North America by 5.5%. Aside from positive top-line results, in H1, smartglasses grew over 200% year-on-year, and growth in H2 is expected to remain elevated, supported by additional product launches. Going down to the P&L, H1 adjusted core operating profit reached €2.53 billion with a margin of 18.1% (Fig. 1), which was aligned with consensus expectation and flat year-on-year on FX-neutral. EssilorLuxottica generated almost €1.8 billion in profit with a margin dilution of 30 basis points compared to H1 2024. This was mainly due to FX and a slight corporate tax rate increase. FCF remains elevated at €951 million, but debt was slightly higher given recent M&A. So, net debt reached €11.3 billion compared to last year at €9.76 billion.

Source: EssilorLuxottica H1 press release - Fig 1

Why are we still neutral?

  1. Q2 sales were 1% below the Wall Street consensus. Incorporating the latest forex update on fiscal year 2025 sales, we reduce our turnover forecast by 2.4% and 1.4% in 2026. We should also include the lower gross margin of the wearable products and their increasing weighting on total top-line sales;
  2. July is seeing a further sales acceleration driven by EMEA and North America. Apparently, there is a capacity constraint on the production of smart glasses. So, EssilorLuxottica needs a higher unit capacity by 2026. While we view this as a positive, we might expect higher D&A going forward. Additionally, the company is considering converting some existing production in Thailand to address the strong demand for smart glasses. Tariffs are a question mark, and H2 profitability will likely see the full impact. At this stage, new CAPEX requirements present a challenge, not in terms of cash outlay, but rather due to uncertainties around the location of the future industrial complex;

  3. While macro sentiment is mixed, the company is viewed as having the best lenses. This is further confirmed by our traction on Varilux, which is ranked best overall and expected to take market share. That said, the company's shares are trading at 37x 2025 and 35x 2026 P/E. This includes turnover and EPS CAGR of 5% and 8%, respectively. Tariffs and inflation created headwinds, but the EBIT margin held steady year-on-year at constant FX, which should help ease concerns that further earnings cuts are imminent. However, we also believe the current P/E will likely go down, leading us to reaffirm a neutral stance. We believe a higher valuation multiple is unsustainable, even considering the recent META investment.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Due to FX, we have lowered our sales forecast to €27.5 billion, compared to a previous projection of €28.5 billion. In our last analysis, we already reduced the EBIT margin on FX, and today, we factor in the lower gross margin of wearable products and their rising share of total revenues, which results in an EBIT estimate of €4.6 billion. With no formal outlook provided, uncertainty remains around the risk of potential earnings downgrades for the 2025 results. As a reminder, EssilorLuxottica provided an outlook at constant exchange rates. Considering an interest payment of €200 million, and a 22% tax rate, our net profit reached €3.4 billion. Considering the current outstanding shares, our EPS is set at €7.4. A current P/E of 37x appears difficult to justify given the 8% CAGR.

Given the current market enthusiasm around the stock, our reduced EPS estimates warrant a lower valuation. However, we maintain a neutral stance and reiterate our target price of $267.4 per share for ESLOF ($133.7 for the ESLOY ADR). Seeking Alpha's D rating on the company's valuation also demonstrates this (Fig. 2).

Fig 2

Risks

EssilorLuxottica's downside risks include: 1) a pricey valuation and a lower guidance might significantly impact its ongoing share price evolution, 2) US tariff implications and CAPEX decisions, 3) changes in customer tastes and consumer slowdowns (the latest smart glasses prices are high versus the usual company's glasses), 4) loss of valuable licensed brands, 5) FX changes and their impact on sales and margins, and 6) higher competition. We removed the approval risks from Stellest since the latest release. With its positioning straddling technology and healthcare, the group faces heightened exposure to sector-specific risks.

Conclusion

Although the H1 results were supportive, especially regarding the growth of smart glasses and a pickup in the USA, we still believe EssilorLuxottica is not correctly priced in. The fundamentals are right, but the current multiple is not justified. We reiterate our neutral view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fifth Third Bancorp: Using Preferred Stock To Position For Lower Interest Rates
data.description:
Introduction

Fifth Third Bancorp (NASDAQ:FITB) is the holding company of the Fifth Third Bank, headquartered in Ohio and operating in that state, as well as Kentucky, Indiana, Michigan, and the Carolinas as main areas of interest.

The bank has a few issues of preferred shares outstanding, and I have been looking at some of those issues with a low preferred dividend to allow for the potential to generate capital gains, as lower interest rates on the financial markets should result in higher preferred share prices (assuming the risk levels of Fifth Third don’t increase).

A Robust Net Interest Income While Keeping Loan Loss Provisions Low

One of the key elements when looking at a bank is, of course, the net interest income, as that traditionally is the main driver behind the earnings performance of most banks that don’t have a substantial investment banking side.

In the case of Fifth Third Bancorp, the bank reported a total interest income of $2.48B in the second quarter of the year, and although that’s a $136M decrease compared to the second quarter of last year, the interest expenses decreased by almost $250M, and this resulted in a net increase of the net interest income by approximately $108M, to just under $1.5B.

As the income statement above shows, Fifth Third reported a $750M non-interest income (with a steadily increasing contribution from wealth and asset management services), while the total amount of non-interest expenses remained in line with the expectations as well. This resulted in a pre-tax and pre-provision income of $981M. After deducting the $173M in loan loss provisions (a rather sharp 75%+ increase compared to the second quarter of last year), the pre-tax income was $808M on a reported basis, resulting in a net profit of $628M.

This means the bank needed just around 6% of its net profit to cover all the preferred dividends, leaving $591M as the net profit attributable to the shareholders. This represents $0.88 per share. The total EPS in the first half of the year was approximately $1.59 per share, a 5% increase, despite recording a 60% higher loan loss provision.

The increase in loan loss provisions isn’t a deal breaker. After all, this bank is running a $210B balance sheet, with a loan portfolio in excess of $122B.

As you can see below, the bank specializes in commercial loans (representing in excess of 40% of the total loan book), with commercial mortgages representing about 10% of the loan book. The consumer loan portfolio is geared towards residential real estate (with the combination of residential mortgages and home equity loans representing almost half of the total consumer loan book).

And as you can see below, the vast majority of the commercial loans are current, with just over 0.3% of the total commercial loan book classified as ‘past due’.

The portfolio of residential mortgages has an average LTV ratio of 74.1%, but approximately 85% of the loans either have an LTV ratio of less than 80% or have mortgage insurance.

And of the almost $2.7B in high-LTV and uninsured mortgages, less than 1% was placed on a non-accruing status. The average LTV ratio in the home equity portfolio was approximately 65.4%.

A Look at a Lower-yield Preferred Security

One of the preferred shares issued by Fifth Third that I have been keeping an eye on is the Series K non-cumulative preferred shares, which are trading with (NASDAQ:FITBO) as the ticker symbol. This series was issued in 2019 and offers a 4.95% preferred dividend yield based on the $25 value per preferred share. These preferred dividends are payable on a quarterly basis, and the $1.2375 in annual preferred dividends represents just under $0.31 per share per quarter.

As FITBO is currently trading at $20.79 per share, the current yield is approximately 5.95%. This security is past its call date (which was in September of last year), which means the Series K preferred shares can be called at any given time. However, considering the cost of equity is just 4.95%, I don’t think Fifth Third is considering calling these securities.

Investment Thesis

I think the 6% current yield offered on the Series K preferred shares offers an attractive risk/reward ratio, as this represents a mark-up of 230 bps and 184 bps over the 5-year and 10-year US treasury rate.

I’m also getting increasingly interested in the common shares of Fifth Third Bancorp, despite those shares trading at just over 2 times the tangible book value. Fortunately, the TBV is slated to get a boost from the AOCI burndown, which will add about $2/share over the next 3.5 years. Additionally, assuming a moderate increase of the dividend in the next 3 years (by 2 cents per quarter per share on an annual basis), the bank will retain at least $5/share in earnings. This would bring the TBVPS to at least $30 by the end of FY 2028, which makes the current high premium versus the tangible book value more palatable.

data.title: Salesforce: The Agentforce Inflection Point Is Coming (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Salesforce, Inc. (NYSE:CRM) is one of the more misunderstood names in the market today. The market appears concerned that topline growth has not yet accelerated. This comes as many software peers have already seen their own acceleration in growth rates. I believe that investors should be patient, as Agentforce may take time to see widespread adoption. Yet with the company using itself as customer zero, I believe that time will come sooner rather than later. The stock is not yet pricing in this inevitable acceleration in growth. I am upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy" rating.

CRM Stock Price

I last covered CRM in August, where I rated it a "Buy" and stated that it is not time to give up on the stock. The stock has underperformed by double digits since then.

Growth has not accelerated as hoped for, but the age of Agentforce may be just beginning.

CRM Stock Key Metrics

CRM is an enterprise software company that, through an aggressive history of M&A, has expanded well beyond its foundations as a customer relationship management (‘CRM’) company to become more of a data platform. That positioning has helped it transition to become an agentic enterprise.

In the most recent quarter, CRM delivered 10% YoY revenue growth to $10.2 billion, slightly exceeding guidance of between $10.11 billion and $10.16 billion. While the market appears to be yawning at the lack of an acceleration in overall growth, I note that the company has seen its flagship data products in Tableau and MuleSoft both see growth accelerate over the last two quarters.

Across various podcasts, CEO Benioff has noted that CRM is “customer zero” and has been able to use Agentforce to replace 4,000 customer support roles and divert those resources towards sales. I have already been expecting solid operating leverage given the high gross margin profile, but I suspect that we might see accelerated gains in profitability moving forward. CRM did indeed deliver solid results on the profit front, with $1.96 in GAAP EPS crushing guidance of between $1.80 and $1.82 and $2.91 in non-GAAP EPS also crushing guidance of between $2.76 and $2.78. The company saw GAAP operating margins jump 370 bps YoY and 300 bps QoQ.

Management paired those strong profitability gains with $2.6 billion in cash returned to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. This is not the same company with the growth-at-any-cost reputation of the past.

CRM ended the quarter with $15.3 billion of cash and $5 billion of strategic investments versus $8.4 billion of debt, representing a solid net cash balance sheet. I note that the pending acquisition of Informatica is likely to bring the company to a leverage-neutral position.

Looking ahead, management has guided for the third quarter to see up to 9% YoY revenue growth.

Management barely touched full-year expectations. I suspect that many investors were disappointed, as they may have been hoping to see growth rates inflect upwards in the second half of this year.

On the conference call, management disregarded notions that generative AI would disrupt the software model, calling it instead “the fundamental extension of SaaS.” Management was optimistic with regard to Agentforce, noting that in their company alone, “about 1.5 million conversations happened directly with these agents and 1.5 million of these conversations happened with humans.” Management outlined a world in which “every prospect is finally getting a call back.” I’m excited to see what this means for their sales execution moving forward, as well as the attractiveness of the product to potential customers. Management was understandably asked if their view on M&A has changed. In my opinion, management has a less-than-stellar track record with regard to M&A, one marred by a seeming lack of care for valuation. I was satisfied with their answer, as they called share repurchases their highest priority, followed by dividends, and only then followed by M&A. Management also appeared to suggest having greater scrutiny with regard to M&A. While management did not raise guidance for growth moving forward just yet, they did note that their pipeline “is growing in the high teens,” with “big deals” growing at nearly a 20% pace. I suspect that it is only a matter of time before the top-line growth engine accelerates again.

Is CRM Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

CRM recently traded hands at around 22x earnings. While that is based on non-GAAP earnings, I note that stock-based compensation has remained stable YoY, and I expect GAAP margins to converge with non-GAAP margins over time.

Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain high single-digit top-line growth over the coming decade.

While one could quibble on whether the company could really sustain such an aggressive growth rate for so many years, I believe that near-term estimates look too conservative, as I expect growth rates to inflect higher. The company looks like a coiled spring and may see growth accelerate meaningfully as it proves out the value of Agentforce. For this reason, I would not focus too much on the lack of acceleration in current and past quarters, as there may be a sudden tipping point in which growth inflects upwards. I continue to expect the business to reach 40% net margins over the long term. That places the current valuation at just around 15x long-term earnings. Given the net cash balance sheet, highly predictable revenue stream, and solid GAAP profitability, the stock looks too cheap. I expect the stock to trade up to 7x to 10x sales over time, equating to a valuation of 17.5x to 25x long-term earnings. The higher end of that range would place it in similar waters as Microsoft (MSFT), which I view as appropriate given the similar wide platform portfolio.

CRM Stock Risks

CRM may see volatility in the event of a market downturn. It is possible that generative AI ends up being a disruptive force rather than a tailwind. Perhaps generative AI leads to mass layoffs, which would hurt seat-based vendors like CRM. Perhaps generative AI allows customers to bring software products in-house. It is possible that management engages in aggressive M&A activity that destroys shareholder value, as in the past.

CRM Stock Conclusion

There will always be concerns about why the company has not yet seen growth accelerate. But, as some peers have shown, sometimes patience is needed to simply meet the tipping point. Salesforce has used Agentforce on itself with great success. I expect rapid customer adoption, which in turn may lead to a surprising acceleration in growth rates. This, combined with the strong balance sheet and profitability, suggests that the stock is too cheap. I am upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy" rating. CRM is a core holding of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio.

data.title: CLOZ Provides Income Stability Amidst Market Uncertainty
data.description:
Overview

As market indexes continue to close near their all-time highs, income funds may be an efficient way to offset the uncertainty and volatility that traditional equities may expose investors to. Now that the US government shutdown has begun, an income fund like Eldridge BBB-B CLO ETF (NYSEARCA:CLOZ) can be a great way to strengthen the stability of your portfolio. When I previously covered CLOZ, I issued a buy rating due to the fund's ability to provide stability in a time of uncertainty.

Looking at the performance over the last 12 months, we can see that CLOZ's share price has remained relatively stable. The ETF did experience a decline throughout the end of March, alongside the rest of the market. This decline was in response to President Trump's initial tariff proposals. Thankfully, CLOZ was capable of riding the momentum of the market rally over the last few months and completely recovered from its prior decline.

When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 8.4% over the same period. CLOZ now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 8%, and distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. These monthly payouts make CLOZ an enticing choice for investors that are seeking the highest level of capital preservation and income reliability.

The fund's portfolio puts an emphasis on floating-rate securities, which means that the sustainability of payouts may shift if interest rates continue to decline. Despite this weakness, CLOZ is still a great defensive choice for investors seeking capital preservation through the market uncertainty. At the same time, investors should still maintain realistic expectations around CLOZ's ability to provide attractive returns when held over a longer time frame. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that CLOZ implements.

Fund Strategy & Risk Profile

According to the fund's latest fact sheet, CLOZ now has total net assets of $796.7M that are spread across 232 different holdings. The fund has a public inception date of January 2023 and has a management fee of 0.5%. Collateralized loan obligations are bundles of loans that have been packaged together and then broken down by rating. As the fund's name implies, CLOZ focuses on CLOs that are rated between BBB and B. The fund's objective is to generate a high income while also preserving capital for investors. The breakdown by rating is as follows:

  • CLO BBB (Investment Grade): 60.5%
  • CLO BB (Below Investment Grade): 36.3%
  • Cash & Equivalents: 3.2%

Just for additional context, I think it's important to understand the history of defaults within the CLO market. S&P Global compiled data that reinforces just how well-built many of these CLO funds are. Based on the data, the level of defaults has been historically low throughout different market environments. The table below is broken down into two separate categories:

  1. CLO 1.0 = mid-1990s to 2008
  2. CLO 2.0 = 2009 to present

The second generation of CLO transactions began following the aftermath of the Great Financial Crisis. Underwriting methods and the rating process became more transparent in an effort to improve the resilience of these assets. For instance, CLOs started to exclude collateral pools in assets other than corporate loans and certain corporate bonds. The details of all defaults are as follows:

As we can see, defaults that occurred within the BBB to B range were very low. While this is a great indication of how well constructed these CLOs are, a low history of defaults doesn't automatically mean that investors should assume that CLOZ's exposure to defaults is low. In a scenario where interest rates remain elevated, CLOZ can still be indirectly impacted by the deterioration of the debt markets.

To mitigate these risks, CLOZ allocates its assets across a diverse range of industries. As we can see below, CLOZ is most heavily allocated to High Tech securities that make up 13.35% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Banking, Finance, Insurance, and Real Estate and Healthcare securities, which accounts for 12.06% and 11.43% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three industries makes up over a third of total assets, so these may have the greatest impact on CLOZ's daily price movement and total return potential.

While it isn't specifically outlined in any of CLOZ's reporting documents, the fund's overview does state that these CLOs are structured on a floating rate basis. Therefore, the level of income that CLOZ can generate can shift based on the interest rate environment. A higher interest rate environment will translate to higher interest income that can be collected from its portfolio of loans.

Although the Fed implemented a small 25 basis point cut in September, the Federal Funds rate still remains near the higher end of its range over the last decade. So I anticipate that CLOZ will be able to continue supporting the higher dividend yield in the near term. In a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward, CLOZ's underlying earnings may decline and cause the distributions to shift, and the dividend yield may decline.

Performance Expectation

Since the fund prioritizes income generation from CLOs, investors should limit their expectations around the ETF's ability to provide a meaningful level of growth when held over time. As stated in the fund's objective, capital preservation is the priority. When assessing the performance since CLOZ's inception, the ETF has demonstrated that its strategy is efficient at preserving capital as it sets out to do. For instance, the fund has slightly increased by 6.16% since inception. When including all distributions paid out, the total return jumps up to 34.54% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of the returns will come from CLOZ's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions.

So who is CLOZ a best fit for? I believe that the fund's strategy is best fit for conservative investors that have a low-risk tolerance to the volatility in the markets. Additionally, CLOZ may be a good fit for retirees that live off the distributions within their portfolio, and seek the highest level of stability and capital preservation. Conversely, younger investors that are trying to maximize total return potential may not be a good fit for CLOZ.

Just to visualize the tradeoffs, we can refer to the performance comparison below. While CLOZ was able to preserve capital throughout the market decline earlier in the year, it has also underperformed more traditional index-tracking ETFs such as SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), on a YTD basis. So by choosing price stability and income generation, you may be actively choosing a lower return over time.

Just as a point of reference, I believe it would be fitting to compare CLOZ's performance against a peer CLO ETF, as well as a CLO ETF that is specifically focused on AAA-rated CLOs. By running a quick comparison, we can determine whether or not CLOZ's exposure to some below investment grade securities is worth the extra risk. I believe Janus Henderson offers two compelling peer CLO ETFs, including:

  • Janus Henderson B-BBB CLO ETF (JBBB): offers a dividend yield of 7.7%
  • Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA): offers a dividend yield of 5.6%

Unfortunately, all of these ETFs have a limited operating window, so a performance comparison is limited. We do not have a frame of reference on how these ETFs may have performed through different market environments over the last decade, so there's a level of uncertainty here. When running a quick return comparison, we can see that CLOZ has outperformed the peer group in both total return and price change. Therefore, CLOZ is a good fit for investors that are okay with the slight exposure to some lower rated CLOs, in exchange for the possibility of higher returns.

Distribution

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1752 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8%. The monthly payouts are great for income investors that prefer the frequent payouts. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that the actual payout amounts can shift every month. Since CLOZ is generating its income from a diverse range of floating rate securities, these shifting payout amounts are a natural symptom of the fund.

There's always a risk that CLOZ's payouts are substantially reduced when interest rates fall back to their pre-pandemic levels. Since the fund launched when interest rates were already elevated, we have no point of reference for how low the payouts can actually fall. So while the fund has performed well in terms of income consistency, this can always change based on how low-interest rates fall.

What I like about CLOZ is that the dividend yield is competitive when compared to other asset classes. For instance, there are many BDCs (Business Development Companies) that offer a parallel dividend yield, such as Main Street Capital (MAIN), which has a dividend yield of 6.7%. However, MAIN's share price has remained much more volatile compared to CLOZ, which may not fit every investor's preference.

When looking at the distributions that were paid to shareholders, the dividends are classified as ordinary income. Ordinary dividends typically have the highest tax burden for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, desired income level, and the amount of capital invested, a position in CLOZ may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset these potential tax impacts.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I still maintain my buy rating on CLOZ at this time. Although the Fed implemented a small 25 basis point cut to rates, the federal funds rate still remains at the higher end of its ten-year history. Therefore, it is very likely that CLOZ will be able to continue offering a competitive dividend yield over the next twelve months. Investors seeking stability and capital preservation may be happy with CLOZ's performance. Looking at the historical data, the amount of defaults within BBB- to B- rated CLOs has remained low, which is reassuring for investors seeking reliable income. Assuming interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months, the probability of defaults is likely to remain low. However, lower interest rates may also lead to lower payouts if the amount of income generated from CLOZ's holdings declines.

data.title: The Trade Desk Becomes A Fat Pitch Yet Again
data.description:
The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) has been one of the more volatile names amidst a volatile market this year. The company appears to have hit a serious bump in the growth story, leading some investors to question if the track record of excellence has come to an end. The company has been hit harder by tariffs than the larger peers, and one might be wondering if we can still expect aggressive growth ahead. The company maintains a strong balance sheet with attractive profit margins. The valuation still looks reasonable even if aggressive growth does not return. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

TTD Stock Price

I last covered TTD in June, where I stuck by my buy rating in spite of acknowledging that the “easy money” has been made. The stock has plummeted 28% since then.

In hindsight, I should have simply downgraded the stock with it trading at the high end of fair value amidst a frothy broader market. But the latest selloff looks like a buying opportunity.

TTD Stock Key Metrics

TTD is one of the largest demand side platforms [DSP] which means that it helps advertisers purchase ads across the entire internet.

The company has a global presence and views its international operations as being an important catalyst for long term growth.

In the most recent quarter, the company delivered 19% YoY revenue growth to $694 million, exceeding guidance of $682 million.

The company generated $271 million in adjusted EBITDA, exceeding guidance of $259 million.

TTD ended the quarter with $1.7 billion of cash versus no debt, which, in conjunction with their solid GAAP profitability, represents a strong balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided for the third quarter to see revenue of at least $717 million, representing 14.2% YoY growth and adjusted EBITDA of around $277 million. I suspect that this implied deceleration in growth took investors by surprise, especially given that the sentiment in the room following the last report was that management had returned to their “beat and raise” ways (or at least that’s what I thought). The fact that revenue growth is now projected to go below 15%, after coming in at 25% in the first quarter, is certainly interesting.

On the conference call, management acknowledged the “real” tariff impact but also their confidence that the uncertain environment may make advertisers more likely to work with an established operator like TTD. TTD noted that they do not view Amazon (AMZN) to be a direct competitor in the advertising space due to it primarily only carrying advertisements for products on its own platform. Management also noted that they believe that they are playing the long game, as the more famous platforms like Alphabet and Meta Platforms (META) are able to drive profits earlier due to the closed-end nature of the ecosystem, but TTD, on the other hand, is tackling a larger market with “way more upside.” I’m not sure I fully agree there, but it could offer an explanation for why TTD, in spite of the smaller revenue base, is not seeing as strong growth as META.

Is TTD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, TTD traded hands at around 29x earnings.

That represents some premium to where META and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) trade, but TTD is projected to post faster growth moving forward.

Those expectations stem in part from TTD’s positioning on “the whole internet” as well as its smaller revenue base. I suspect that consensus estimates are likely too optimistic, as they imply no deceleration and even some acceleration from 2025 levels. That said, I can still see this name sustaining at least 10% to 13% top-line growth over the coming years. Based on my projection for 35% net margins over the long term, the stock trades at around 26x long term earnings. That is arguably a reasonable valuation given the net cash balance sheet, GAAP profitability, and long-term growth runway. Even after the fall since my last report, I still do not see too much of a case for multiple expansions unless the company can accelerate growth on a sustained basis.

TTD Stock Risks

TTD might be facing some disruption in the advertising ecosystem. It is possible that, as AMZN takes more and more market share from e-commerce, it is also able to take share in advertising even if it is not technically a direct competitor to TTD in the literal sense. It is possible that generative AI leads to the creation of new products that rival TTD but at a cheaper cost point. TTD is not dirt cheap and may face great volatility if it turns out that it is not deserving of a premium multiple. This, I think, is the biggest risk, as the market might not be ready for slower growth ahead, even if I am.

TTD Stock Conclusion

TTD offers double-digit growth, GAAP profitability, and a net cash balance sheet with the potential for accelerating growth. The near term appears cloudy due to tariffs and potential economic volatility. The company still looks positioned to power the shift towards online advertising. TTD shares many of the traits I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Rivian's Sales Problems Continue
data.description:
When it comes to the electric vehicle space, a lot of names have not lived up to the hype over the years. One of the names I've been most disappointed in has been Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN), as the company has been unable to grow its vehicle deliveries meaningfully over the past couple of years. On Thursday, the company announced its Q3 production and delivery figures while providing a mostly negative update to its full-year outlook. As a result, shares are taking a hit, and the latest news might set the stock up for a poor finish to 2025.

Previous Coverage Of The Name

It was roughly a month ago that I last covered Rivian, detailing how short interest in the stock had just reached a new all-time high. As the company continued to dilute investors over time and consumers waited for the R2 platform to launch next year, bearish bets had been steadily rising. Since that article, shares have declined by almost 3%, compared to a more than 4% gain in the S&P 500.

The Q3 Vehicle Figures

At the company's Q2 report, management reiterated its guidance for deliveries this year to be in the range of 40,000 to 46,000 vehicles. As a reminder, Rivian sold over 50,000 units back in 2023, so this guidance range called for the number to be even less two years later. The company was slated to see a bit of extra competition during Q3, as US electric vehicle makers rushed to increase sales ahead of the expiring federal tax credit.

For the third quarter, the company produced 10,720 vehicles and delivered 13,201 vehicles, which was in line with the company’s forecast. This was the best quarter of 2025 in terms of deliveries for Rivian; however, the production number was only the second-highest quarter. The company's main facility will be shut down for a time in the back half of this year for upgrades as it gets ready for the R2 launch next year.

Unfortunately, it was the guidance update that was surprisingly negative. For 2025, Rivian now expects to deliver 41,500 to 43,500 vehicles. While the bottom end of the guidance range was hiked by 1,500 vehicles, the top end was lowered by 2,500 vehicles. Thus, the new midpoint is 500 vehicles lower than the guidance from just a few months ago. Despite launching some new vehicle variants this year, it is clear that there is a sales problem here.

Back in late 2021, analysts were calling for almost $28 billion in revenues this year for the company and more than $38 billion in 2026. Now, the average estimates for this year and next stand at just $5.29 billion and $7.02 billion, respectively. It wouldn't surprise me if the average for this year ticks down slightly, given the latest guidance update, although the 2026 average has risen about $50 million since my prior article. We'll get full Q3 results from the company on November 4th.

Another Potential Capital Raise

The company finished Q2 with roughly $7.5 billion in cash and short-term investments. There was also $4.4 billion in debt on the balance sheet, leaving just over $3 billion in net cash. The cash pile has been helped in recent quarters by the equity investments made by Volkswagen. Rivian will get another roughly $2.5 billion from VW in the following three tranches:

  • $1 billion equity expected in 2026 upon successful winter testing of certain Volkswagen Group vehicles.

  • $1 billion loan expected in October 2026.

  • $460 million equity expected to be issued at the earlier of January 3, 2028, or the start of the first production of a saleable Volkswagen Group vehicle using the joint venture’s technology.

Rivian also has access to a loan worth up to $6.6 billion that relates to its buildout of its new factory in Georgia, unless the Trump administration decides to go after that in its anti-clean energy push. In the near term, Rivian may decide to raise further capital as it potentially burns through more cash and gears up for the R2 launch, as only $1 billion more can potentially come from VW in the next year. As I mentioned previously, the outstanding share count is already at a new all-time high, topping 1.2 billion in late July, up from less than 900 million in March 2022. That share count will rise further with the next VW equity deal, and of course, ongoing stock-based compensation is diluting investors over time.

A Look At Current Valuations

Right now, Tesla (TSLA) goes for about 13.1 times its expected sales for 2026, but the rest of the EV players all go for much less. As the chart below shows, Rivian currently trades below the valuation of Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID), but above all the other names in the space, including VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS). Chinese players like NIO Inc. (NIO), BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF), ZEEKR (ZK), and XPeng Inc. (XPEV) go for way less at the moment, while Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (PSNY) also trades for way less than the EV group average. All valuations are as of Thursday's close.

If I exclude Tesla as the dramatic outlier, Rivian seems quite a bit overvalued. The stock finished Thursday trading at just 2.33 times its expected revenues for 2026, before any coming analyst estimate changes, significantly higher than the 1.26 average of the other seven names. The average price target of $14.26 currently represents more than 5.5% upside. However, the street's average has come down by more than $3 over the past year and is well off the peak of over $130 over three years ago, so analysts have had to cut their valuation models significantly over time.

Final Thoughts And Recommendation

While Rivian announced Q3 deliveries that were in line with its projections, the company narrowed its full-year guidance range, mostly towards the downside. The company clearly has a sales problem, as it hasn't been able to top 2023's yearly delivery record, with 2025 now slated to see deliveries well below the company's peak, and it likely will be well into 2026 before the R2 platform becomes material to the business.

With the stock continuing to trade at a sizable premium to most electric vehicle names, I have to continue my sell rating today. I'm guessing analyst estimates are about to take another leg lower, and perhaps Rivian will need to raise more capital to help with the R2 launch. As sales troubles continue and investors get diluted further over time, this is not a stock I want to be in for the next quarter or two.

data.title: PSQ: Shorting The Nasdaq-100 For Downside Protection
data.description:
An Introduction to the ProShares Short QQQ ETF

ProShares Short QQQ ETF (NYSEARCA:PSQ), as the name clearly says, is a way to short the Nasdaq-100 representative Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ). One look at the price action this year would put off the most optimistic investor, but learning more about it reveals a tactical utility that can be used to take advantage of downtrends in QQQ, a tech-heavy, no-financials-focus instrument that generally shows clear trend reversals. I shall not be making any recommendation on PSQ, as this is for educational purposes only.

Understand QQQ to Appreciate PSQ

If one asks the question, "How many major drawdowns has the QQQ experienced in the last 30 years?" and then plots that on a graph, this is what one would see.

One simply cannot ignore the fact that QQQ's drawdowns have been getting sharper and more frequent in the last five years than in the past twenty-five. This is the downward momentum that PSQ aims to capture, and by using a derivatives strategy that involves swaps and futures, it targets a daily return of -1x. Said differently, if QQQ falls on any given trading day, PSQ should rise by the same percentage that QQQ fell; at least, that is what it has been designed to do.

This is a rather innocuous-sounding description that hides a considerable amount of risk, and this risk arises from a unique characteristic of having one trading day as its time window to achieve that goal. It is then repeated the following trading day, having now reset to zero at the new price level.

The risk with this strategy should be fully appreciated because it emphasizes what is already a mathematical dilemma for investors. To elaborate, I shall pose a question that I wish more investors would ask themselves:

If one owned a $100 asset that appreciated 10% on the first day and depreciated 10% on the next, would that be a break-even position, a net loss position, or a net gain position?

Many investors would say that was a break-even deal. Unfortunately, they would be wrong. A 10% gain on the first day brings your asset value up to $110; 10% down from there is not $100, it is $99. Since the asset value changed at the end of the first day, the 10% loss on the second day is on the $110, not the original $100.

This is where the risk primarily comes from. Over a longer holding period, the cumulative depreciation could turn out to be much higher than one would have thought, and this is why PSQ's price chart looks like this…

… and its returns across different time windows look like this…

This perfectly brings out the risk of holding PSQ for long periods. It has consistently shown negative returns since its inception and continues to do so on a one-month basis (ended August). With QQQ now up 27% in the last six months alone, there are no prizes for guessing how PSQ may perform over the next few weeks or months.

This is not a recommendation of any kind, but I urge readers not to draw conclusions from this one risk aspect alone. This ETF is not broken, in simple words. It is not intended to be a long-term holding, and the fund's material reiterates this several times. However, it serves an excellent tactical function when QQQ buyer momentum gets saturated and the fund gaps down and bounces away from the 14-period RSI overbought region above 70.

We shall return to the suggested investment approach for PSQ, but we have one more stop to make before we go there—how is an inverse result achieved using derivative instruments?

Figuring Out the Role of Derivatives

Having understood the implications of a daily reset, which incidentally is how any asset's net value works over time, I shall now elaborate on the strategy being used.

In their absolute essence, swaps and futures are nothing more than agreements and contracts that involve two counterparties who agree to exchange the difference in returns on two individual cash flows. Now, these may be cash flow delta, differences in two agreed-upon rates of interest, or the divergence between a floating and a fixed rate. The underlying asset is the NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX). It is against this index that the swaps are priced, and for running this as an active strategy, the ETF charges an expense ratio of 0.95%.

Funds do not generally share the granular bits of their strategy, such as the agreed-upon rates, but for an investor, the important part of this is that PSQ benefits when QQQ has a negative return on a trading day. Unfortunately, since the whole tech sector seems to rally more often than it recedes, the fund tends to lose more money than it makes, hence its negative price return.

PSQ's Worst Enemy: QQQ Appreciating

Do remember that PSQ also pays a dividend, and at this ETF share price level the yield is up near the 6% mark as of the trailing twelve months. The problem here, as with many inverse funds, is that the dividend payouts are not from an organically growing asset base but a declining one.

For the last reported fiscal year, the fund realized a loss of $228 million, which is much higher than the combined gains of about $132 million from investment interest (U.S. Treasuries, primarily) and appreciated assets, giving it a net operational asset loss of $96 million. Capital transactions accounted for a net positive change of about $134 million and helped offset the realized loss.

The only way PSQ was able to pay dividends, which are essentially coming out of new share issues, was by absorbing the loss. The net change of $4 million in asset value is nothing more than the difference between what the fund collected from new share issuances, less the cost of redeemed shares, less the distribution amount, and less the operational asset loss.

This is now quite disturbing because, as of the last annual report, the fund reported back-to-back losses of considerable magnitude. FY 2024 seems even worse than FY 2025 because the fund was unable to issue more shares than it redeemed; more importantly, it not only recorded higher realized losses but also much lower unrealized asset appreciation.

That's the danger with derivatives, and if one looks at the swaps currently held, one should see ample risk moving forward.

One can see the net effect of unrealized asset losses on the Nasdaq-100 Index swaps with expirations from November 2025 through April 2027. The fund is essentially underwater with this strategy.

The Prudent Way to Use PSQ

Although it does not seem suitable for long-term investors, PSQ is more of a scalpel-like instrument, not a bludgeoning one. Viewed through that lens, it is a highly utilitarian ETF designed to capture short-term downside from QQQ.

To reveal this attractive side, I shall showcase how PSQ performed during periods when QQQ was in a downward swing. Remember, QQQ itself is quite volatile (about 23 percent annualized), which means there are plenty of one-day and multi-day opportunities where PSQ can make a meaningful contribution to your portfolio.

The green line in the top half of the image above represents PSQ. As QQQ moved sideways and then started to decline around the second half of February, the entire period through to the end of April was a good time for PSQ, which appreciated 18%, as opposed to QQQ's 16% decline. In the lower part of the above image, one can see clearly where there was a trend reversal when QQQ bounced off the oversold RSI level. This will have been your cue to exit the fund.

That is the best-case scenario for a fund that's short on technology, and its function is limited to that. This means it can be the part of your portfolio that helps offset declines in your main QQQ or tech holdings, but it is much wiser to sell off when you see a reversal at oversold and then buy in again and hold until the bounce off the overbought—and so on and so forth.

I sincerely hope this helps set the pace for a more thorough look at PSQ, a largely misunderstood hedging instrument for tech investors.

This article answers these three main questions about PSQ:

  • What is the best way to use PSQ?
  • Is PSQ designed for short-term trading or long-term holding?
  • Are there specific risks with PSQ that investors need to be aware of?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: U.S. Real Estate Sector Report - Fall 2025
data.description:
Sector conditions and outlook

Key:

Current condition

Outlook

APARTMENT

The apartment sector is experiencing a surge in demand through the halfway point of 2025. Vacancy rates have declined sharply in the past 12 months and supply pipelines are seeing significant declines in new projects. Higher interest rates, as well as material and labor costs are the primary factors. Although rental growth has remained pressured, the sector is poised to see stronger operational performance over the balance of this year.

HOTEL

Hotel sector performance remains nuanced, largely due to policy uncertainty and declining savings rates among middle-income households. While travel data has been supportive and occupancy levels have stayed relatively healthy, ongoing caution around the economic outlook and policy-related headwinds continue to constrain more robust sector performance.

INDUSTRIAL

The industrial sector is facing headwinds associated with strong new supply in select metros and tenant uncertainty surrounding the impact of tariffs, which is complicating longer-term leasing conditions. Despite these challenges and higher vacancies, the sector remains sought after by both equity investors and lenders. Sector performance will be determined by both trade flows and economic growth, which we believe will drive continued leasing for larger and more modern warehouse facilities.

OFFICE

Although office fundamentals show signs of stabilization, they remain weak due to a lack of hiring in traditional office-using occupations and a lack of traction in the return to the workplace. Vacancy rates remain elevated in the low-19% range as leasing activity remains soft. Liquidity within the sector has been mixed with some improvement in sales volume, but it has been limited to portfolio level activity and core assets, which are trading selectively. Leasing economics remains a challenge for owners as the pricing pendulum continues to tilt toward tenants. Limited new supply will help in the nascent recovery, but only for the most well positioned assets.

RETAIL

The retail sector continues to exhibit strong performance and has become more sought after by institutional investors due to outperformance in 2024. The sector’s unique position as a diversified sector continues to attract investors toward its cyclically resistant formats, which are also more defensively positioned against e-commerce penetration. Consumer resilience, however, is being tested as evidenced by a flattening of sentiment in the specter of higher inflation, which could erode discretionary spending power below all but the top income groups.

SINGLE- FAMILY RENTAL

The single-family rental (SFR) sector has performed well over the past 12 months but faces modest headwinds in select metros due to overdevelopment, which has tempered projected rental growth. Vacancy rates in most regions remain near equilibrium, and stable demand continues to support the sector’s income profile.

DATA CENTERS

Data center demand remains strong as new demand continues to outpace the pace of new supply. At present the top-10 U.S. markets have less than 10MW of power available, which equates to a vacancy rate of roughly 2%. The number of tenants in space continues to expand powered by both cloud computing and AI related companies. Hyperscalers and intermediaries are leasing power to satisfy short-term AI contracts.

STUDENT HOUSING

The student housing sector continues to exhibit solid fundamentals. However, it is becoming more competitive as much of the demand is now being met. Increased migration to southeastern states has generated optimism, particularly with students opting to stay closer to home. Despite these positive trends, we remain cautious about the sector due to moderating enrollment trends at four-year institutions.

LIFE SCIENCES

Fundamentals in the life sciences sector remain under pressure following a wave of overdevelopment and limited new space supply. Nationwide R&D lab vacancies, according to CBRE, reached 22.7% as of mid-year despite an increase in total leasing activity. However, net absorption remained negative due to lease expirations. The sector continues to face stress from persistently soft post-pandemic venture capital funding trends.

Source: Principal Real Estate, September 2025.

Apartment

Sector overview

The apartment sector is experiencing a surge in demand through the halfway point of 2025. Vacancy rates have declined sharply in the past 12 months and supply pipelines are seeing significant declines in new projects. Higher interest rates, as well as material and labor costs are the primary factors. Although rental growth has remained pressured, the sector is poised to see stronger operational performance over the balance of this year.

Private equity

Following a challenging 2024, apartment occupancy and rents are showing improvement, signaling a potential turning point driven by stronger demand and a slowdown in new supply across most regions. The vacancy rate has declined for the first time in two years, and rental growth is beginning to accelerate as market conditions move back toward equilibrium. On the operational side, Q2 trade-outs were positive for both renewals and new leases. Expense ratios have also improved over the past two quarters, supported by rising revenue and stabilized costs.

Many of the headwinds that weighed on the sector over the past two years are now fading. Overdevelopment—concentrated in the Sunbelt—has slowed meaningfully as higher capital costs constrain new starts. This easing in supply comes just as net demand has reached its strongest pace in several years.

That said, performance continues to vary widely by metro and submarket, with a broad gap between the strongest and weakest markets. In addition, expanding supply from the single-family rental (SFR) and build- to-rent (BTR) sectors is creating added competition for apartments, particularly in larger floorplan segments.

Private debt

Apartment lending remains appealing to many lenders who favor the property sector based on housing shortages nationally, challenging economics for first- time home buyers, favorable long-term trends and robust market liquidity. Strong tenant demand has effectively countered new supply in most markets over the past year, making lenders more confident about placing money in apartment loans. Although the lending was down nearly 20% during 2025 Q2 versus the year-prior period, excluding several large portfolio transactions apartment lending was 7% higher during Q2 2025 than the year-prior period.

Government-sponsored entities (GSEs) are currently offering the lowest spreads for most stabilized core apartment properties – sometimes in the low 100 basis point (BPS) range for low-to-moderate leverage facilitated by the GSE’s low cost of capital. Insurance company and bank pricing for core apartment commercial mortgage loans (CML) tend to be closer in the 125 to 150 BPS range over UST yields for loan-to-values (LTV) between 50% and 65%. LTVs in the high-60s to mid-70s % may be offered by the GSEs and CMBS conduits with spreads in the high 100 to low 200 BPS range, while debt funds sometimes offer “stretch senior” apartment loans up to 80% LTV with pricing in the high 200's over SOFR.

Regarding loan proceeds, the GSEs and CMBS lenders both allow borrowers to “buy down” interest rates at closing to improve debt service coverage ratios (DSCR), thus allowing borrowers to increase proceeds for loans that would otherwise be DSCR-constrained.

REITs

Apartment REITs have significantly underperformed against the broader REIT index year-to-date in 2025. Market sentiment has deteriorated following recent earnings releases, primarily due to two factors: the peak leasing season ended earlier than usual, and a slowdown in the job market has hindered the expected recovery following heavy supply. The wide affordability gap between buying and renting continues to favor the rental market, helping maintain stable occupancy levels and steady renewal rates. However, investors are increasingly concerned about a potential decrease in pricing power during the second half of the year, which could impact growth projections for 2026.

Both Coastal and Sunbelt REITs have disappointed investors with their revenue trends. Coastal REITs failed to meet high expectations, while Sunbelt REITs showed slower-than-anticipated recovery. However, lower property tax and insurance expenses have partially offset these challenges. Coastal markets are projected to maintain stronger organic growth, as Sunbelt markets continue to work through their supply pipeline. Currently, Apartment REITs are trading at a mid-teen discount compared to consensus estimates.

CMBS

The GSEs remain the dominant lenders in the multifamily sector, with $146 billion in approved lending capacity for 2025. However, agency issuance has been constrained by low transaction volumes. Multifamily exposure in conduit and single-asset single-borrower (SASB) CMBS, while smaller in scale, is still significant at roughly $67.5 billion outstanding. Over the past five years, multifamily’s share of conduit issuance has ranged from 22.6% in 2021 to 23.8% in 2025.

Loan performance in the fixed-rate segment remains solid. The conduit multifamily delinquency rate stands at 4.77%, the second lowest among property sectors, trailing only industrial. By contrast, some weakness is emerging in floating-rate SASB loans originated at tight debt yields during the low-interest-rate environment. Rising rates have sharply increased debt service burdens, constrained NOI growth, and created valuation pressures that complicate refinancing. As a result, the SASB multifamily delinquency rate has climbed to 8.0%, driven primarily by maturity defaults.

Overall, multifamily remains a favored sector within fixed-rate conduit CMBS. Longer loan terms, fixed-rate coupons, consistent NOI growth, limited affordability in the for-sale housing market, and relatively low exposure to rent-regulated properties all continue to support its relative strength.

Hotel

Sector overview

Hotel sector performance remains nuanced, largely due to policy uncertainty and declining savings rates among middle-income households. While travel data has been supportive and occupancy levels have stayed relatively healthy, ongoing caution around the economic outlook and policy-related headwinds continue to constrain more robust sector performance.

Private debt

Debt funds have been the most active hotel lenders in recent months with some hotel debt also supplied by CMBS conduits, CMBS SASB, insurance companies and banks. Overall hotel lending has been down in 2025 due to a dearth of large SASB transactions (relative to 2024 activity), but a healthy volume of smaller hotel loans continues to be originated.

Debt funds generally prefer LTV ratios of 60% to 65% or less for hotels but occasionally extend as high as 75%. Their credit spread over SOFR tends to range between the high 200 BPS range for the best-of-the- best hotel loans, to the more common low-to-mid 300 BPS range, to spreads in the mid-to-high 400 range with LTVs at the higher end of the acceptable range or for more transitional properties. Debt funds frequently determine pricing requirements based on a combination of property cash flow, loan basis, market, asset quality, business plan, and sponsorship.

CMBS single asset single borrower (SASB) lenders target hotel loans more than $200-250 million. Today they may offer 50% to 70% LTV financing with spreads between 200 to 300+ BPS over SOFR. CMBS conduit pricing is similar. CMBS sources tend to focus heavily on cash flow with less willingness to consider transitional assets than debt funds will consider.

Banks have recommended originating a small volume of hotel construction loans but typically limit LTVs to 40% to 50% with significant structural requirements, including interest carry reserves and recourse. Spreads for non-recourse hotel construction loans range between the 500 and 800 BPS range based on leverage, project quality and sponsorship.

REITs

Lodging REITs have underperformed the broader REIT market year-to-date, primarily due to slowing RevPAR (Revenue Per Available Room) projections for late 2025 and 2026, coupled with persistently high operating expenses. Market performance shows distinct variation across segments, with luxury hotels maintaining solid mid-single digit growth vs. midscale and economy hotels posting low-single-digit declines. This divergence reflects stronger consumer dynamics in the luxury segment, where travelers maintain spending power through higher savings and net worth, while budget-conscious consumers and businesses have shifted toward more affordable accommodations. Industry forecasters, including STR, expect this trend to continue through 2026. However, given a broader slowdown in key lodging macro drivers such as business capex, disposable income growth, international inbound travel, and weaker government bookings, higher end hotels are not expected to be fully immune from sector-wide uncertainty.

Recent months have shown decreased business transient demand, though airline companies and hotel brands report improving booking trends for the fall season. Group travel remains a projected leader into 2026, supported by previously booked high-rate contracts, despite slowing near-term booking activity. While corporations and associations maintain future booking activity, government-related group demand remains weak. The leisure travel segment shows mixed performance, with the luxury segment maintaining resilience with minimal price sensitivity, while the lower-end market shows increased price sensitivity and demand softness. This divergence in performance across segments and travel types suggests a complex market environment heading into 2026, helping explain the group trading at roughly 10% discounts to NAV, with clear advantages for REITs focused on higher-end properties despite broader market uncertainties.

CMBS

Despite a slowdown in demand from post-COVID highs, hotel operations continue to perform well, with loan performance improving accordingly. The conduit hotel delinquency rate, which peaked near 20% in July 2020, has declined to 5.95%—1.28 percentage points below the overall conduit delinquency rate. Leisure travel initially led the recovery in destination markets, followed by a gradual rebound in business travel.

Hotel operators have sustained RevPAR growth largely by raising average daily rates, even as occupancy remains below pre-pandemic levels, particularly in higher-end categories. That said, year-over-year RevPAR growth is beginning to stabilize as consumers have largely drawn down excess savings.

CMBS issuance in 2020 and 2021 included minimal hotel exposure due to underwriting challenges and investor caution. As fundamentals have improved, hotel contributions have risen to 11% of 2024 conduit issuance and 10.6% of year-to-date 2025 issuance. Still, hotel performance remains highly sensitive to economic conditions. In a slower-growth environment where discretionary spending softens, the sector could face renewed pressure—underscoring the need for cautious underwriting and forward-looking credit assessments.

Industrial

Sector overview

The industrial sector is facing headwinds associated with strong new supply in select metros and tenant uncertainty surrounding the impact of tariffs, which is complicating longer-term leasing conditions. Despite these challenges and higher vacancies, the sector remains sought after by both equity investors and lenders. Sector performance will be determined by both trade flows and economic growth, which we believe will drive continued leasing for larger and more modern warehouse facilities.

Private equity

The industrial sector is entering a new phase of more nuanced performance across most markets. Overdevelopment in key port locations, combined with increased tariff uncertainty, has made leasing dynamics more difficult to assess and created headwinds for net absorption in certain regions. Coastal hubs such as the Ports of Los Angeles and New York/New Jersey have experienced rising vacancy rates. New supply is expected to outpace demand again in 2025; however, projects under construction are declining rapidly due to pricing and underwriting pressures, which should allow the sector to regain a measure of equilibrium in 2026.

On a more positive note, new industrial leases continue to feature healthy contractual rent steps—often exceeding 3% annually, compared with sub-2% escalations in most other sectors. In addition, the number of markets where rents surpass $10 per square foot has expanded significantly and is now the norm for Class A assets across most top-50 metros.

Even so, the outlook remains more moderate due to elevated construction in recent years and shifting sentiment tied to policy uncertainty. While the sector remains highly sought after by investors, fundamentals are now balanced at a point where market rent growth is expected to taper to inflationary levels—or below—in the near term.

Private debt

Lenders continue to aggressively pursue loans on industrial properties despite tariff-induced uncertainty affecting tenant demand in several key port markets. Still-positive national net absorption, a slower pace of new construction activity, still-healthy vacancy rates, and moderate forecast rental rate growth in most markets continue to bolster lender confidence in the property sector.

Well-located, high-quality warehouses and large logistics properties in major markets today often garner 7- to 10-year fixed rate financing from insurance companies and banks with 50% to 65% LTV ratios and spreads over U.S. Treasury yields near 130 to 150 basis points (producing interest rates in the low- to mid- 5% range). Higher leverage is often available from CMBS conduits and debt funds at somewhat higher spreads and rates.

REITs

Industrial REITs have outperformed the REIT index year-to-date, partially recovering from their 2024 underperformance. While initial optimism for second-half rent growth improvement faded after a sharp post-Liberation Day leasing slowdown, sentiment has recently improved as market conditions exceeded feared scenarios. Despite slow tenant decision-making, REITs anticipate pent-up demand activation, with potential catalysts including tariff clarity and Fed rate cuts. Operating results continue decelerating but have exceeded expectations, leading to raised 2025 guidance reflecting both year-to-date outperformance and previous conservative outlooks.

The slower operating environment has reduced development starts, while higher capital costs are limiting acquisition opportunities. REITs are trading at mid- to high-single-digit discounts to NAV, reflecting macro-uncertainty and tariff volatility. These discounted valuations have attracted investor interest, evidenced by an unsolicited private equity bid for a small-cap industrial REIT and activist involvement in a Southern California-focused REIT.

CMBS

Industrial loans currently carry the lowest delinquency rate in the CMBS universe at 1.05%, underscoring the sector’s resilience relative to other traditional property types. While credit quality and investor demand remain strong, recent tariff implementations have introduced temporary headwinds. Trade-related disruptions have pressured supply chains and tenant operations, creating short-term uncertainty for leasing and rent growth. Even so, fundamentals remain solid, supported by long-term e-commerce adoption and sustained logistics demand.

Since 2020, the CMBS SASB market has supplied more than $43.7 billion in floating-rate debt to industrial owners, providing an efficient vehicle for financing large portfolio deals. Blackstone was a leading participant in early 2024, though a broader set of borrowers has since accessed the market for portfolio financing.

Conduit CMBS industrial allocations have normalized to the 6% to 7% range, consistent with pre-pandemic levels, after rising to approximately 10% in recent years. Conduit loans are typically smaller than SASB deals and often collateralized by properties in tertiary locations, with less functional layouts and non-investment-grade tenancy (frequently via sale-leasebacks). As a result, underwriting standards for conduit industrial loans have generally been more conservative than for SASB loans.

Overall, the sector continues to benefit from historically strong NOI growth, resilient cash flows, and supportive investor sentiment, positioning it as one of the most stable components of the CMBS market.

Office

Sector overview

Although office fundamentals show signs of stabilization, they remain weak due to a lack of hiring in traditional office-using occupations and a lack of traction in the return to the workplace. Vacancy rates remain elevated in the low-19% range as leasing activity remains soft. Liquidity within the sector has been mixed with some improvement in sales volume, but it has been limited to portfolio level activity and core assets, which are trading selectively. Leasing economics remains a challenge for owners as the pricing pendulum continues to tilt toward tenants. Limited new supply will help in the nascent recovery, but only for the most well positioned assets.

Private equity

Despite stabilization in vacancy rates, tenants continue to return space to the market—even upon lease renewals. Many occupiers are still rightsizing their office footprints to align with utilization metrics that remain well below pre-pandemic levels. Inducement costs such as free rent, tenant improvements, and delayed start dates remain elevated, posing challenges for owners. Capitalization on new leases is at a generational high, reflecting weak tenant leverage and ongoing uncertainty. Outside of a handful of top-tier new buildings in major markets, rents are largely flat, though record pricing continues to be set at the high end of the market.

Adding to the sector’s challenges is a slowing labor market. Office-using employment is no longer driving a recovery in demand, as corporations have paused hiring amid near-term economic uncertainty and are slow to backfill open positions. The health of the labor market is being closely monitored, given its potential implications for future office demand.

Private debt

Despite ongoing stress in the national office market, select high-quality properties with strong leasing fundamentals and experienced sponsorship have attracted limited lender interest from insurance companies. This activity remains sparse, typically requiring spreads about 75 basis points above comparable industrial or apartment loans of similar credit risk and tenor. Still, 2025 has marked the cautious reentry of some insurers into office lending. Underwriting standards are conservative, with a focus on the durability of property cash flow rather than net operating income, and loan-to-value ratios often capped around 50%.

CMBS lenders have also returned on a constrained basis, as the CMBS bond market generally permits up to 20% office exposure per issuance. Within that framework, conduit lending is largely reserved for higher-quality assets supported by solid in-place leasing.

Certain debt funds have expressed willingness to finance a broader range of office properties, though required yields frequently exceed what property cash flows can support. Class B and C buildings, assets with occupancy below 80%, or properties with weighted average lease terms under five to seven years, continue to attract little to no lender interest. As a result, most office transactions still rely on existing lenders refinancing their own loans or sellers providing financing to facilitate dispositions.

REITs

Office REITs have underperformed the broader REIT index year-to-date. While fundamentals are gradually improving, this has not yet translated into meaningful gains in occupancy or earnings growth. Cash flows remain under pressure as higher leasing activity drives elevated capex. Even so, REIT portfolios continue to benefit from the flight-to-quality trend, materially outperforming the broader office market.
